mnemonics.title: Chomsky Hierarchy Order
mnemonics.content: Remember with: 'Reg-u-lar Con-text-Free Con-text-Sen-sitive Un-res-tricted' - the number of letters increases as power decreases (3,2,1,0). Think: R3 C2 C1 U0 = Reg-3, Contex-2, Contex-1, Unrestricted-0.

mnemonics.title: Regular Grammar Rules
mnemonics.content: Remember: 'Left OR Right, one terminal at most, partner at the end or nothing' - regular grammars have at most one non-terminal and it appears only at the beginning (left-linear) or end (right-linear) of the production.

mnemonics.title: Formal vs Natural Languages
mnemonics.content: FOSS: Formal = Ordered, Structured, Synthetic; Natural = Organic, Spontaneous, Social. Or: Formal = Math (precise), Natural = Human (fluid).

acronyms.usage: Grammar where left side has single non-terminal, used for programming languages
Simplest grammar type, recognized by finite automata
AI field dealing with understanding and generating human languages
Automaton with stack memory that recognizes context-free languages
Simple machine with finite states recognizing regular languages

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a pyramid: Top (wide) = Unrestricted (most powerful), Middle = Context-sensitive, Next = Context-free, Bottom (narrow) = Regular (most restricted). Each level is a subset of the one above.
Draw an upside-down tree: Root = Start symbol, Branches = Production rules applied, Leaves = Terminal symbols (the actual string). Like a family tree showing ancestry of the sentence.
Circle = state, Arrow with symbol = transition. Draw as a flowchart: circles are decision points, arrows show paths. Finite = small number of circles, Pushdown = circles with a stack next to them.
Think of restrictions as 'guards': Regular = single guard at end; Context-free = one guard anywhere; Context-sensitive = multiple guards that must be followed; Unrestricted = no guards.

analogies.analogy: Formal languages are like chess rules - precise, unambiguous, same everywhere. Natural languages are like spoken conversations - flexible, contextual, can have double meanings.
Finite automaton is like a simple vending machine with fixed buttons (states). Pushdown automaton is like that vending machine with a storage shelf (stack) - it can remember previous selections.
Parser is like a grammar teacher checking if a sentence follows all grammar rules and explaining its structure, not just checking if it 'sounds right'.
Natural language ambiguity is like a blurry photograph - you can see multiple possible images. Formal language is like a crystal-clear photo - exactly one interpretation.
